---
title: "How Git Works, Visually"
subtitle: "The mental model behind commits, branches, and merges"
author: "Pranjal Rawat"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: black
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center: true
    navigation-mode: linear
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #16171a; --fg: #e6e6e9; --muted: #8b8b92; --sub: #a3a3ab;
  --accent: #7cb0de; --feat: #6bbf8a; --amber: #d9a441; --rule: #2a2c31;
  --node: #1d2733; --edge: #5a6472; --ink: #0f1114;
}
.reveal { font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif; }
.reveal, .reveal .slides section { background: var(--bg); color: var(--fg); }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3 { color: var(--fg); font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: -0.5px; text-transform: none; }
.reveal h2 { font-size: 1.55em; border-bottom: 2px solid var(--rule); padding-bottom: 0.25em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; }
.reveal a { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal .slide-number { background: none; color: var(--muted); }
.reveal .footer, .reveal .slide-number { font-size: 12px; }
.reveal strong { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal em { color: var(--sub); font-style: italic; }
.reveal .subtle { color: var(--sub); font-size: 0.8em; }
.reveal .lead { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.5; }
.reveal code { color: var(--feat); background: rgba(124,176,222,0.08); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px; }
.reveal section img { background: none; border: none; box-shadow: none; }
/* SVG shared visual language */
.gitfig { width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 62vh; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.node { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; }
.node.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.node.merge { fill: #3a2f14; stroke: var(--amber); }
.node.ghost { fill: #191c20; stroke: var(--edge); }
.hash { fill: #cfe3f3; font: 600 16px ui-monospace, "SF Mono", Menlo, monospace; text-anchor: middle; }
.hash.dim { fill: var(--muted); }
.msg { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap { fill: var(--fg); font: 16px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap.big { font-size: 21px; font-weight: 600; }
.zonelabel { fill: var(--muted); font: 600 14px -apple-system, sans-serif; letter-spacing: 1.5px; text-anchor: middle; }
.edge { stroke: var(--edge); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrow); }
.flow { stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrowA); stroke-dasharray: 6 5; }
.tag { rx: 5; }
.tag.main { fill: var(--accent); }
.tag.feat { fill: var(--feat); }
.tag.head { fill: var(--amber); }
.tagtext { fill: var(--ink); font: 700 14px ui-monospace, monospace; text-anchor: middle; }
.leader { stroke-width: 2; fill: none; }
.leader.main { stroke: var(--accent); }
.leader.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.leader.head { stroke: var(--amber); }
.zone { fill: none; stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 12; }
.card { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 8; }
.card.hot { stroke: var(--amber); }
.cardtext { fill: var(--fg); font: 15px ui-monospace, monospace; }
.note { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.9); transition: all 0.3s ease; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in.visible { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); }
/* draw-on edges: a parent pointer draws itself from child to parent when revealed.
   pathLength="1" makes stroke-dasharray:1 span the whole path at any length. */
.edge.draw, .flow.draw { stroke-dasharray: 1; stroke-dashoffset: 1; }
.reveal .fragment.visible .edge.draw,
.reveal .fragment.visible .flow.draw,
.reveal .edge.draw.fragment.visible,
.reveal .flow.draw.fragment.visible { animation: gv-draw 0.55s ease both; }
@keyframes gv-draw { from { stroke-dashoffset: 1; } to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
/* commit node pops in about its own centre */
.reveal .fragment.pop { opacity: 0; transform-box: fill-box; transform-origin: center; }
.reveal .fragment.pop.visible { animation: gv-pop 0.45s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.8,0.2,1) both; }
@keyframes gv-pop { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.5); } 60% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1.06); } 100% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); } }
</style>

<svg width="0" height="0" style="position:absolute" aria-hidden="true">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5a6472"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowA" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#7cb0de"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

# The problem, and the fix {.center}

::: lead
Every result you trust rests on one question: *exactly which version of the code and data produced it?*
:::

Git is the machine that gives that question an answer.

::: notes
This deck shows the mental model, not the commands. By the end you should be able to picture what git is doing when someone says commit, branch, merge, or rebase.
:::

---

## Life without version control

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 430" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment"><rect class="card" x="120" y="60" width="300" height="46"/><text class="cardtext" x="140" y="89">analysis.py</text></g>
  <g class="fragment"><rect class="card" x="150" y="120" width="300" height="46"/><text class="cardtext" x="170" y="149">analysis_final.py</text></g>
  <g class="fragment"><rect class="card" x="180" y="180" width="300" height="46"/><text class="cardtext" x="200" y="209">analysis_final_v2.py</text></g>
  <g class="fragment"><rect class="card hot" x="210" y="240" width="320" height="46"/><text class="cardtext" x="230" y="269" style="fill:var(--amber)">analysis_final_REALLY.py</text></g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="720" y="150">Which one is real?</text>
    <text class="note" x="720" y="192" text-anchor="middle">Who changed it last, and why?</text>
    <text class="note" x="720" y="220" text-anchor="middle">Can I get the old version back?</text>
    <text class="note" x="720" y="248" text-anchor="middle">Two people edited at once,</text>
    <text class="note" x="720" y="272" text-anchor="middle">one set of edits vanished.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the folder-of-dated-spreadsheets problem every data scientist has felt. Version control exists to answer all four of these questions at once.
:::

---

## What version control gives you

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 380" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="60" y="90" width="270" height="200"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="195" y="150" style="fill:var(--accent)">History</text>
    <text class="note" x="195" y="192" text-anchor="middle">every version kept,</text>
    <text class="note" x="195" y="218" text-anchor="middle">with who and why</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="365" y="90" width="270" height="200"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="150" style="fill:var(--feat)">Recovery</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="192" text-anchor="middle">return to any past</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="218" text-anchor="middle">state, a mistake is</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="244" text-anchor="middle">never permanent</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="670" y="90" width="270" height="200"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="805" y="150" style="fill:var(--amber)">Collaboration</text>
    <text class="note" x="805" y="192" text-anchor="middle">many people change</text>
    <text class="note" x="805" y="218" text-anchor="middle">one project in</text>
    <text class="note" x="805" y="244" text-anchor="middle">parallel, safely</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

For a data scientist, these three add up to one word: **reproducibility**.

::: notes
History, recovery, collaboration. The payoff for our work specifically is reproducibility: a result is only trustworthy if you can say exactly which version produced it.
:::

---

## A commit is a snapshot

The unit of git. One commit records three things.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="gitfig">
  <circle class="node" cx="500" cy="200" r="52"/>
  <text class="hash" x="500" y="206">a1b2c3</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader main" d="M 500 148 L 500 92" style="stroke:var(--edge)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="76">a snapshot of every file, right now</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader main" d="M 552 200 L 720 200" style="stroke:var(--edge)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="815" y="195">a message:</text>
    <text class="note" x="815" y="222" text-anchor="middle">"why this change"</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node ghost" cx="245" cy="200" r="40"/>
    <text class="hash dim" x="245" y="205">9f2e</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 448 200 L 288 200"/>
    <text class="cap" x="245" y="128">a pointer to</text>
    <text class="cap" x="245" y="152">its parent</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Snapshot plus message plus parent pointer. The parent pointer is what turns a pile of commits into a history.
:::

---

## Commits form a chain

Each commit points back to the one before it. That is the whole history.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 360" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <text class="msg" x="220" y="150">init</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="220" cy="200" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="220" y="206">a1b</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 458 200 L 262 200"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <text class="msg" x="500" y="150">add model</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="500" cy="200" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="500" y="206">9f2</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 738 200 L 542 200"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <text class="msg" x="780" y="150">fix bug</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="780" cy="200" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="780" y="206">c73</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="tag head" x="742" y="262" width="76" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="780" y="283">latest</text>
    <path class="leader head" d="M 780 240 L 780 262"/>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Arrows point from a commit back to its parent. The newest commit is where "latest" sits. Add a commit and the chain just grows to the right.
:::

---

## The hash is a fingerprint

Each commit's ID is computed from its contents. Change the past and every ID downstream changes with it.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 380" class="gitfig">
  <circle class="node" cx="220" cy="150" r="42"/>
  <text class="hash" x="220" y="156">a1b</text>
  <circle class="node" cx="500" cy="150" r="42"/>
  <text class="hash" x="500" y="156">9f2</text>
  <path class="edge" d="M 458 150 L 262 150"/>
  <circle class="node" cx="780" cy="150" r="42"/>
  <text class="hash" x="780" y="156">c73</text>
  <path class="edge" d="M 738 150 L 542 150"/>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="220" y="250" style="fill:var(--amber)">edit this commit...</text>
    <path class="leader head" d="M 220 205 L 220 232" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node merge" cx="220" cy="150" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="220" y="156" style="fill:var(--amber)">e04</text>
    <circle class="node merge" cx="500" cy="150" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="500" y="156" style="fill:var(--amber)">77a</text>
    <circle class="node merge" cx="780" cy="150" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="780" y="156" style="fill:var(--amber)">b19</text>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="320" style="fill:var(--amber)">...and every ID after it changes too</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

That is how git guarantees the history has not been quietly tampered with.

::: notes
The hash is a fingerprint of everything that came before. This tamper-evidence is a free side effect of the parent-pointer design.
:::

---

## How to read the diagrams that follow {.center}

Many of the diagrams from here on are a **photograph of a real git repository**. A command is run, and git's actual state is read back and drawn.

A commit is a **circle**. An arrow runs to its **parent**. A colored box is a **branch** (just a pointer). `HEAD` marks where you stand. An **amber** ring is the commit the last command just made.

Use the arrow keys. Each step is one command, and the graph grows as you go.

<!--FB:what-git-is-->

<!--FB:your-first-commit-->

<!--FB:seeing-your-history-->

<!--FB:ignoring-files-->

<!--FB:what-a-commit-is-->

## A change moves through three places

You edit, you choose what to record, then you record it.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 430" class="gitfig">
  <rect class="zone" x="40" y="120" width="270" height="210"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="175" y="150">WORKING DIRECTORY</text>
  <rect class="zone" x="365" y="120" width="270" height="210"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="150">STAGING AREA</text>
  <rect class="zone" x="690" y="120" width="270" height="210"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="825" y="150">HISTORY</text>

  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card hot" x="70" y="215" width="210" height="54"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="92" y="248">model.py  (edited)</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 314 240 L 361 240"/>
    <text class="note" x="337" y="200" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">add</text>
    <rect class="card" x="395" y="215" width="210" height="54"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="417" y="248">model.py</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 639 240 L 686 240"/>
    <text class="note" x="662" y="200" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">commit</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="825" cy="242" r="42"/>
    <text class="hash" x="825" y="248">a1b</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="390" text-anchor="middle">Staging lets you record one clean, logical change even when the working directory is messy.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Working directory, staging area, history. The staging step is the part people forget: it is what lets you commit a tidy logical change out of a half-finished mess.
:::

---

## Distributed: every copy holds it all

Clone a project and you copy the entire history to your machine, not just the latest files.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 440" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="50" y="95" width="390" height="250"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="245" y="128">YOUR CLONE  ·  LOCAL</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="235" r="30"/><text class="hash" x="150" y="241" style="font-size:14px">a1b</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="245" cy="235" r="30"/><text class="hash" x="245" y="241" style="font-size:14px">9f2</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="340" cy="235" r="30"/><text class="hash" x="340" y="241" style="font-size:14px">c73</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 215 235 L 180 235"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 310 235 L 275 235"/>
    <text class="note" x="245" y="300" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">full history, works offline</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="560" y="95" width="390" height="250"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="755" y="128">REMOTE  ·  GITHUB</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="660" cy="235" r="30"/><text class="hash" x="660" y="241" style="font-size:14px">a1b</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="755" cy="235" r="30"/><text class="hash" x="755" y="241" style="font-size:14px">9f2</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="850" cy="235" r="30"/><text class="hash" x="850" y="241" style="font-size:14px">c73</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 725 235 L 690 235"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 820 235 L 785 235"/>
    <path class="flow" d="M 445 175 L 555 175"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="160" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">push</text>
    <path class="flow" d="M 555 300 L 445 300"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">pull</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="410" text-anchor="middle">The remote is just another full copy. It is special only because everyone agrees to sync there.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the heart of git's design. There is no single authoritative server the way older centralized tools had. Every clone is complete.
:::

---

## git is not GitHub

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="70" y="90" width="360" height="240"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="250" y="150" style="fill:var(--accent)">git</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="195" text-anchor="middle">the tool</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="228" text-anchor="middle">commits, branches, full history</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="261" text-anchor="middle">on your machine, works offline</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="570" y="90" width="360" height="240"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="750" y="150" style="fill:var(--feat)">GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="195" text-anchor="middle">a service built on top</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="228" text-anchor="middle">web UI, access control, review</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="261" text-anchor="middle">the automation in the next cards</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="370" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">You can use git with no service at all. The real history lives in every clone.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A distinction worth keeping straight in any conversation. git is the tool; GitHub is a host built on it. Torvalds wrote git in 2005 to manage the Linux kernel after BitKeeper's free terms were withdrawn.
:::

<!--FB:the-three-trees-->

<!--FB:clone-fetch-push-pull-->

# Branches, and how they rejoin {.center}

::: lead
A branch is a separate line of work: a movable pointer that lets you build without disturbing the main line.
:::

---

## A branch is just a movable label

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="gitfig">
  <text class="note" x="140" y="90">main</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="200" cy="180" r="38"/><text class="hash" x="200" y="186">a1b</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="400" cy="180" r="38"/><text class="hash" x="400" y="186">9f2</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 362 180 L 238 180"/>
    <rect class="tag main" x="362" y="238" width="76" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="400" y="259">main</text>
    <path class="leader main" d="M 400 218 L 400 238"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="600" cy="180" r="38"/><text class="hash" x="600" y="186">c73</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 562 180 L 438 180"/>
    <rect class="tag main" x="562" y="238" width="76" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="600" y="259">main</text>
    <path class="leader main" d="M 600 218 L 600 238"/>
    <path d="M 438 253 C 490 285, 512 285, 560 258" style="fill:none;stroke:var(--amber);stroke-width:2;stroke-dasharray:5 4;marker-end:url(#arrowA)"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="315" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">commit again, the label moves forward</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="365" text-anchor="middle">Creating a branch just writes down one more pointer. That is why branching is nearly free.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The whole trick: a branch is a name pointing at a commit. Commit on it and the pointer advances. No files are copied.
:::

---

## The fork: work in isolation

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="70" y="290">main</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="180" cy="280" r="36"/><text class="hash" x="180" y="286">a1b</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="380" cy="280" r="36"/><text class="hash" x="380" y="286">9f2</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="580" cy="280" r="36"/><text class="hash" x="580" y="286">c73</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 344 280 L 216 280"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 544 280 L 416 280"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="470" y="95" style="fill:var(--feat)">feature</text>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="560" cy="130" r="36"/><text class="hash" x="560" y="136">d4e</text>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="760" cy="130" r="36"/><text class="hash" x="760" y="136">b81</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 540 160 L 400 250"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 724 130 L 596 130"/>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="365" text-anchor="middle">You split off a private line. main keeps moving without you.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Almost every change a modern team ships starts life on a branch like this. The main line stays stable while the work happens off to the side.
:::

---

## Merge: tie the lines together

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 420" class="gitfig">
  <text class="note" x="60" y="290">main</text>
  <text class="note" x="470" y="95" style="fill:var(--feat)">feature</text>
  <circle class="node" cx="170" cy="280" r="34"/><text class="hash" x="170" y="286" style="font-size:14px">a1b</text>
  <circle class="node" cx="360" cy="280" r="34"/><text class="hash" x="360" y="286" style="font-size:14px">9f2</text>
  <circle class="node" cx="550" cy="280" r="34"/><text class="hash" x="550" y="286" style="font-size:14px">c73</text>
  <path class="edge" d="M 326 280 L 204 280"/>
  <path class="edge" d="M 516 280 L 394 280"/>
  <circle class="node feat" cx="540" cy="130" r="34"/><text class="hash" x="540" y="136" style="font-size:14px">d4e</text>
  <circle class="node feat" cx="730" cy="130" r="34"/><text class="hash" x="730" y="136" style="font-size:14px">b81</text>
  <path class="edge" d="M 522 158 L 384 252"/>
  <path class="edge" d="M 696 130 L 574 130"/>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node merge" cx="860" cy="280" r="40"/><text class="hash" x="860" y="286" style="fill:var(--amber)">M</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 820 280 L 584 280"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 832 262 L 762 150"/>
    <text class="cap" x="860" y="360" style="fill:var(--amber)">merge commit</text>
    <text class="note" x="700" y="405" text-anchor="middle">M has two parents. The history honestly records the split and the rejoin.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A merge commit has two parents, one from each branch. Merging is usually automatic; git stitches together changes to different files or different parts of a file on its own.
:::

---

## Rebase: replay onto a straight line

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 470" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="60" y="150" style="fill:var(--muted)">before</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="180" cy="150" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="180" y="156" style="font-size:13px">a1b</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="320" cy="150" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="320" y="156" style="font-size:13px">9f2</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="460" cy="150" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="460" y="156" style="font-size:13px">c73</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 292 150 L 208 150"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 432 150 L 348 150"/>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="420" cy="60" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="420" y="66" style="font-size:13px">d4e</text>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="560" cy="60" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="560" y="66" style="font-size:13px">b81</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 405 84 L 335 130"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 532 60 L 448 60"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 500 200 L 500 275"/>
    <text class="note" x="540" y="245" style="font-size:14px">git rebase</text>
    <text class="note" x="60" y="365" style="fill:var(--muted)">after</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="365" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="150" y="371" style="font-size:13px">a1b</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="290" cy="365" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="290" y="371" style="font-size:13px">9f2</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="430" cy="365" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="430" y="371" style="font-size:13px">c73</text>
    <circle class="node merge" cx="570" cy="365" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="570" y="371" style="font-size:12px;fill:var(--amber)">d4e'</text>
    <circle class="node merge" cx="710" cy="365" r="28"/><text class="hash" x="710" y="371" style="font-size:12px;fill:var(--amber)">b81'</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 262 365 L 178 365"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 402 365 L 318 365"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 542 365 L 458 365"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 682 365 L 598 365"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="440" text-anchor="middle">Linear and tidy, but the replayed commits are rewritten (new IDs). Rebase only work you have not shared.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Rebase rewrites your branch to look as if it were built on the latest main. Cleaner to read, but it changes commit IDs, so the rule is: rebase only your own un-shared work.
:::

---

## A merge conflict is not an error

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 430" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card feat" x="90" y="90" width="330" height="110" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="255" y="122" style="fill:var(--feat)">FEATURE BRANCH</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="120" y="170">line 12:  rate = 0.05</text>
    <rect class="card" x="580" y="90" width="330" height="110" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="745" y="122" style="fill:var(--accent)">MAIN BRANCH</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="610" y="170">line 12:  rate = 0.08</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 255 205 L 420 270"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 745 205 L 580 270"/>
    <rect x="340" y="270" width="320" height="90" rx="8" style="fill:#3a2f14;stroke:var(--amber);stroke-width:2"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="308" style="fill:var(--amber);font-weight:700">CONFLICT on line 12</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="338" text-anchor="middle">git stops and marks the spot</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="405" text-anchor="middle">The same line changed two ways. Git refuses to guess which wins, so a human decides.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Conflicts happen when two branches change the same lines incompatibly. They are more frequent and more painful the longer a branch lives apart, which is the practical argument for keeping branches short.
:::

<!--FB:your-first-branch-->

<!--FB:a-branch-is-a-pointer-->

<!--FB:fast-forward-vs-merge-->

<!--FB:rebase-and-what-it-rewrites-->

<!--FB:anatomy-of-a-conflict-->

# Working as a team {.center}

::: lead
The mechanics of branching are settled. What teams argue about is the *workflow*: when to branch, how long a branch lives, when to merge.
:::

---

## Three workflows on one spectrum

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 440" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="175" y="95" style="fill:var(--accent)">git-flow</text>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--accent)" d="M 60 160 L 300 160"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--muted)" d="M 60 240 L 300 240"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="90" cy="160" r="14"/><circle class="node" cx="270" cy="160" r="14"/>
    <circle class="node ghost" cx="90" cy="240" r="14"/><circle class="node ghost" cx="180" cy="240" r="14"/><circle class="node ghost" cx="270" cy="240" r="14"/>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="180" cy="120" r="14"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 180 226 L 180 134"/>
    <text class="note" x="175" y="300" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">elaborate: long-lived</text>
    <text class="note" x="175" y="324" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">develop + master, plus</text>
    <text class="note" x="175" y="348" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">feature / release / hotfix</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="95" style="fill:var(--feat)">trunk-based</text>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--accent)" d="M 380 200 L 620 200"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="400" cy="200" r="14"/><circle class="node" cx="470" cy="200" r="14"/><circle class="node" cx="540" cy="200" r="14"/><circle class="node" cx="610" cy="200" r="14"/>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="505" cy="160" r="12"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 505 188 L 505 170"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="300" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">lean: one shared line,</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="324" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">integrated many times a day,</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="348" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">branches last hours</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="825" y="95" style="fill:var(--amber)">GitHub flow</text>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--accent)" d="M 700 200 L 950 200"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="725" cy="200" r="14"/><circle class="node" cx="925" cy="200" r="14"/>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="825" cy="150" r="14"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 812 162 L 745 190"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 838 162 L 905 190"/>
    <rect class="tag head" x="787" y="112" width="76" height="26"/><text class="tagtext" x="825" y="130" style="font-size:12px">PR</text>
    <text class="note" x="825" y="300" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">the common default:</text>
    <text class="note" x="825" y="324" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">one main, a short branch,</text>
    <text class="note" x="825" y="348" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">a pull request to merge</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="415" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">Match the workflow to how you release, not to fashion. Driessen himself now points continuous-delivery teams to the simpler end.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Git-flow (Driessen, 2010) suits software shipped as separately supported versions. Trunk-based is what high-performing teams tend to use. GitHub flow sits in between and is the everyday default. In 2020 Driessen added a note that panaceas do not exist.
:::

---

## The pull request

Where a branch is reviewed, tested, and let into `main`.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 400" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="30" y="150" width="200" height="90" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="130" y="200" style="fill:var(--feat);font-size:18px">feature branch</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 233 195 L 283 195"/>
    <rect class="card" x="286" y="150" width="200" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="386" y="200" style="font-size:18px">open a pull request</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 489 195 L 539 195"/>
    <rect class="card" x="542" y="150" width="210" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="647" y="192" style="font-size:17px">review + checks</text>
    <text class="note" x="647" y="220" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px;fill:var(--amber)">the CI pipeline runs here</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow" d="M 755 195 L 805 195"/>
    <rect class="card" x="808" y="150" width="170" height="90" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="893" y="200" style="fill:var(--accent);font-size:18px">merge to main</text>
  </g>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle">Cheap branching is what made this possible: every change proposed, reviewed, and tested before it joins the main line.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the cultural payoff of cheap branching. The automated checks in the third box are continuous integration, covered in the sibling card.
:::

---

<!--FB:pull-requests-->

<!--FB:forks-->

<!--FB:merge-squash-or-rebase-->

## The one tradeoff that runs backwards

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 420" class="gitfig">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="60" y="110" style="fill:var(--muted)">main</text>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none" d="M 120 120 L 640 120"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="120" r="16"/><circle class="node" cx="640" cy="120" r="16"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 210 70 L 590 70"/>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="210" cy="70" r="14"/><circle class="node feat" cx="590" cy="70" r="14"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 190 82 L 165 108"/>
    <path d="M 604 82 L 628 108" style="stroke:var(--amber);stroke-width:3;fill:none"/>
    <path d="M 640 120 l 18 -10 l -8 16 l 20 2 l -16 12 l 10 16 l -20 -8 l -6 18 l -8 -18 l -18 8 l 8 -16 z" style="fill:var(--amber);opacity:0.9"/>
    <text class="note" x="700" y="115" style="fill:var(--amber);font-size:15px">merge hell</text>
    <text class="note" x="380" y="165" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">long-lived: feels safe, drifts far, ends in a giant painful merge</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="60" y="300" style="fill:var(--muted)">main</text>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none" d="M 120 310 L 640 310"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="310" r="16"/><circle class="node" cx="400" cy="310" r="16"/><circle class="node" cx="640" cy="310" r="16"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 470 265 L 545 265"/>
    <circle class="node feat" cx="470" cy="265" r="13"/><circle class="node feat" cx="545" cy="265" r="13"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 455 277 L 415 298"/>
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--feat)" d="M 558 277 L 625 298"/>
    <text class="note" x="380" y="355" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:14px">short-lived: feels risky, but each tiny merge is trivial to resolve</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

Small, frequent merges beat one big one. This is the logic behind trunk-based development and feature flags.

::: notes
The counterintuitive bit: the safe-feeling long-lived branch is the dangerous one. The useful question in a review is simply, how long has this branch been alive?
:::

---

## Four ways teams work, on real repos {.center}

The workflow is not the mechanics. It is the set of rules a team agrees on: when to branch, how long a branch lives, when to merge. Four common shapes, each on a real repository.

<!--FB:direct-to-main-->

<!--FB:github-flow-->

<!--FB:trunk-based-->

<!--FB:gitflow-->

<!--FB:choosing-a-workflow-->

<!--FB:parallel-work-no-conflict-->

<!--FB:same-line-conflict-->

<!--FB:stacked-branches-->

<!--FB:stale-branch-->

## You can almost always undo {.center}

Git rarely loses committed work. A commit that is no longer on any branch still sits in the object store, and the reflog remembers every place `HEAD` has been. The next diagrams show the real safety net: reset, revert, restore, cherry-pick, and the reflog that recovers a "lost" commit.

<!--FB:undo-reset-revert-restore-->

<!--FB:reflog-the-safety-net-->

<!--FB:cherry-pick-and-backport-->

<!--FB:hotfix-and-revert-->

## When many people, and many agents, share a repo {.center}

Notebooks and large data files fight git's line-by-line model. Parallel work, by people or by coding agents, needs its own hygiene: worktrees for isolation, and care with a shared checkout.

<!--FB:notebooks-data-and-lfs-->

<!--FB:worktrees-for-parallel-agents-->

<!--FB:the-shared-checkout-hazard-->

<!--FB:agent-commit-and-review-hygiene-->

## The words you will actually hear

```{=html}
<div style="display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:20px 48px;margin-top:12px;font-size:20px;line-height:1.5">
  <div>
    <div style="color:var(--muted);font-size:14px;letter-spacing:1.5px;margin-bottom:10px">THE CORE</div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--accent)">commit</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">a recorded snapshot, with a message and a parent</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--accent)">hash</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">the fingerprint that identifies a commit</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--accent)">staging area</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">the changes marked for the next commit</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--accent)">clone</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">a full local copy, history and all</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--accent)">remote</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">another copy you sync with</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--accent)">push / pull</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">send commits up / bring commits down</span></div>
  </div>
  <div>
    <div style="color:var(--muted);font-size:14px;letter-spacing:1.5px;margin-bottom:10px">BRANCHING</div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--feat)">branch</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">a movable pointer, a line of work</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--feat)">main</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">the line treated as the source of truth</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--feat)">merge</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">join two lines, recording a merge commit</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--feat)">rebase</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">replay commits for a linear history</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--feat)">merge conflict</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">same lines changed two ways, a human decides</span></div>
    <div><b style="color:var(--feat)">pull request</b> <span style="color:var(--sub)">a proposal to merge, where review happens</span></div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: notes
This is the real payoff of the whole deck. Knowing these twelve words is what lets you follow, and join, an engineering conversation about git.
:::

---

## The whole idea, in one line

::: {.r-fit-text}
A chain of snapshots,
:::

::: {.r-fit-text}
each pointing at its parent.
:::

Everything else, branches, merges, rebases, remotes, is bookkeeping on top of that.

::: notes
If someone remembers one thing, it should be this. The commit graph is the object; all the commands are just ways of moving pointers around it.
:::

---

## Go deeper

- [Pro Git, 2nd edition](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2) - Scott Chacon and Ben Straub. The canonical book, free online.
- [What is Git? the three states and the distributed model](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Getting-Started-What-is-Git%3F)
- [A successful Git branching model (git-flow)](https://nvie.com/posts/a-successful-git-branching-model/) - Vincent Driessen
- [Trunk Based Development](https://trunkbaseddevelopment.com/) and [GitHub flow](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/using-github/github-flow)

::: {.subtle}
Built from the version-control notes in a software-engineering-basics collection.
:::

::: notes
Pro Git is the single best source and it is free. The workflow debate is best read straight from Driessen and the trunk-based site.
:::
